name: 911 Put Options
description: The '9/11 Put Options' theory posits that individuals or organizations with foreknowledge of the September 11th attacks profited from trading put options on the stocks of airlines (United and American), financial institutions (Morgan Stanley, Merrill Lynch), and other companies negatively impacted by the attacks. Put options grant the holder the right to sell a stock at a predetermined price, becoming profitable when the stock price declines. Proponents argue that unusually high volumes of put options were purchased on these stocks in the days leading up to 9/11, suggesting insider trading based on advanced knowledge of the impending attacks. The theory gained traction through online forums and documentaries, often cited as evidence of government or corporate involvement in the attacks. Mainstream investigations, including those by the 9/11 Commission and the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), investigated these trading patterns. They found that while some unusual trading activity did occur, the vast majority of the trading was unrelated to individuals with prior knowledge of the attacks. The 9/11 Commission Report concluded that 'the volume of trading in airline stocks before September 11 was not remarkable' and that investigations into specific trades did not reveal evidence of insider trading related to the attacks. Many of the put options were traced back to institutional investors and were consistent with their normal trading patterns. Critics point out that large-scale put option purchases are not uncommon in volatile markets and that attributing them solely to foreknowledge of 9/11 is speculative and lacks definitive proof.
subtitle: Profiting from tragedy or market coincidence?

claims.claim: Unusual trading volumes of put options before 9/11 indicate foreknowledge.
claims.evidence: Reported spikes in put option purchases on specific stocks.
claims.criticism: Mainstream investigations found no direct link between the trading and individuals with prior knowledge of the attacks. Trading volumes were not consistently unusual and many trades were attributable to normal market activity.

claims.claim: The profits from these trades were used to fund the 9/11 attacks or support associated entities.
claims.evidence: Circumstantial evidence linking certain trades to individuals or groups suspected of involvement in terrorism (often speculative).
claims.criticism: No conclusive evidence supports this claim. Investigations have not found a direct financial trail linking the put option profits to the planning or execution of the 9/11 attacks.

claims.claim: The SEC intentionally suppressed investigations into the put option trading to protect those involved.
claims.evidence: Allegations of inadequate investigation and selective reporting by the SEC.
claims.criticism: The SEC conducted multiple investigations into the trading activity and publicly released their findings. While some critics argue the investigations were insufficient, there is no credible evidence of intentional suppression of evidence.

keyFigures.name: Michael Ruppert
keyFigures.role: Investigative journalist who popularized the theory

keyFigures.name: 9/11 Commission
keyFigures.role: Investigated the trading activity and refuted claims of insider trading

keyFigures.name: Max Keiser
keyFigures.role: Financial analyst who promoted the theory

mythologyConnections: The idea of powerful entities manipulating events for personal gain echoes themes of divine intervention and hidden agendas in various mythologies.

timeline.event: Initial reports and investigations into unusual put option trading emerge
The 9/11 Commission Report is released, addressing and largely dismissing the put option theory

sources.author: Michael Ruppert
sources.work: Crossing the Rubicon: The Decline of the American Empire at the End of the Age of Oil
sources.title: Michael Ruppert

sources.author: Kevin R. Swift
sources.work: Did Unusual Option Market Activity Predict the Terrorist Attacks of September 11, 2001?
sources.title: Kevin R. Swift
